name: Cartagena
summary: Cartagena is a sunny Caribbean city in Colombia surrounded by huge stone walls built to keep pirates out! The colorful old town is a UNESCO World Heritage site, packed with horse-drawn carriages, bright houses, hidden tunnels and a giant fortress called San Felipe.
funFacts: UNESCO named Cartagena's walled Old Town a World Heritage site in 1984. Spanish builders worked for almost two centuries to finish 11 km of stone walls protecting the city.
In 1586 the English pirate Sir Francis Drake sailed in, captured Cartagena, and refused to leave until the Spanish paid him a giant ransom of 107,000 gold ducats.
Castillo San Felipe de Barajas is the biggest fortress Spain ever built in the Americas. Sneaky tunnels twist underneath so soldiers could hear enemy footsteps echoing toward them.
Las Bovedas were 23 thick vaulted rooms used for gunpowder, then as scary dungeons where prisoners stood in seawater. Today they are cheerful little shops selling crafts and souvenirs.
Famous Colombian author Gabriel Garcia Marquez, who won the Nobel Prize, lived in Cartagena as a young reporter and later built a home tucked inside the walled Old City.
thingsToDo: Walk along the top of the old stone city walls, peek out at the Caribbean Sea, and explore Plaza Santo Domingo with its chubby Botero bronze statue.
Climb the zigzag ramps of Castillo San Felipe de Barajas, then duck into the spooky echoing tunnels where Spanish soldiers once hid waiting for attackers.
Browse the Las Bovedas vault shops for handmade gifts, then wander the rainbow streets of Getsemani under the famous floating umbrellas of Calle de las Sombrillas.
Visit the free Museo del Oro Zenu to see hundreds of shiny gold ornaments crafted by the Zenu people who lived here long before Spanish ships arrived.
Sip fresh limonada de coco and try sweet cocadas or panelitas from the candy stalls under Portal de los Dulces beside the Plaza de los Coches clock tower.
Hop a fast boat to the Rosario Islands national park to snorkel above bright coral reefs and tropical fish at one of Colombia's only marine parks.

localWord.meaning: Hello in Spanish, the language everyone speaks here

images.alt: Cartagena's old walled city sits beside the bright blue Caribbean Sea with red rooftops and tall modern buildings nearby.
A huge stone castle called Castillo de San Felipe rises on a hill with tunnels and ramps for soldiers from long ago.
A bright wooden balcony in Cartagena spills over with pink and purple flowers above a sunny street.
The yellow Torre del Reloj clock tower is the main gateway into Cartagena's old walled city.
A pretty colonial street in old Cartagena shows colorful buildings, balconies, and cobblestones from long ago.
